evidence_quotes.quote: These games are often sold with an undeclared expiration date, i.e. they are presented as a digital goods that are bought to outright own, but will be rendered unusable or be "killed" if the publisher decides that required online or authentication services are not profitable anymore.
I suggest requiring that games sold need to either declare a clear end of life or, better, are required to have a clear "end of life" plan to ensure the games are playable forever.

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act provides a perfect opportunity to take care of a problem in the space of digital games / video games / computer games. These games are often sold with an undeclared expiration date, i.e. they are presented as a digital goods that are bought to outright own, but will be rendered unusable or be "killed" if the publisher decides that required online or authentication services are not profitable anymore. I suggest requiring that games sold need to either declare a clear end of life or, better, are required to have a clear "end of life" plan to ensure the games are playable forever. See also the "Stop Killing Games" initiative and the European Citizens' Initiative Stop Destroying Videogames for more details. I myself am a author/designer of video games and support this initiative from both the side of a produces as well as a consumer of digital artifacts.
